title: Login with valid credentials returns a bearer token
preconditions: A registered, active user account exists.
steps: Send POST /auth/login with a valid username and password in the request body.
expectedResult: Status 200. Response body contains a non-empty 'token' field and a 'user' object with at least 'id' and 'username'.

title: Login with invalid credentials is rejected
preconditions: A registered, active user account exists.
steps: Send POST /auth/login with the correct username and an incorrect password.
expectedResult: Status 401. Response body contains an error message that does not reveal whether the username exists.

title: Protected endpoint rejects requests with no Authorization header
preconditions: None.
steps: Send GET /api/testcases with no Authorization header.
expectedResult: Status 401. Response body contains an error message indicating authentication is required.

title: Protected endpoint rejects a malformed token
preconditions: None.
expectedResult: Status 401. Response body indicates the token is invalid.

title: Protected endpoint rejects an expired token
preconditions: A previously-valid token exists but has passed its expiry time.
steps: Send GET /api/testcases with the expired token.
expectedResult: Status 401. Response body indicates the token has expired (distinct message from 'invalid token' if the API differentiates).

title: A valid token for a low-privilege user is rejected on an admin-only endpoint
preconditions: A valid token exists for a user without delete permission.
steps: Send DELETE /api/testcases/{id} using a valid token belonging to a read-only user.
expectedResult: Status 403. Response body indicates insufficient permissions. The resource is not deleted.

title: Create a new test case with valid data
preconditions: Caller has a valid token with write permission.
steps: Send POST /api/testcases with a valid body: { title, priority, status: 'draft' }.
expectedResult: Status 201. Response body contains the created resource with a generated 'id' and a 'createdAt' timestamp, and echoes the submitted fields.

title: Retrieve an existing test case by id
preconditions: A test case with a known id exists.
steps: Send GET /api/testcases/{id} for the known id.
expectedResult: Status 200. Response body matches the previously created/known resource.

title: List all test cases with pagination
preconditions: More test cases exist than fit on a single page.
expectedResult: Status 200. Response body is an array of at most 20 items plus pagination metadata (total count, current page).

title: Update an existing test case's status
preconditions: A test case with a known id exists and is in 'draft' status.
steps: Send GET /api/testcases/{id} to re-fetch the resource.
expectedResult: PUT returns status 200 with the updated resource. The subsequent GET reflects status 'active', confirming the update persisted.

title: Delete a test case and confirm it no longer exists
preconditions: A test case with a known id exists.
steps: Send GET /api/testcases/{id} for the same id.
expectedResult: DELETE returns status 200 or 204. The subsequent GET returns status 404, confirming the resource was actually removed and not just flagged client-side.

title: Updating a test case that does not exist returns 404, not a silent success
preconditions: The given id does not correspond to any existing test case.
steps: Send PUT /api/testcases/does-not-exist-id with a valid body.
expectedResult: Status 404. No resource is created as a side effect.

title: Creating a test case with a missing required field returns 422
preconditions: Caller has a valid token with write permission.
steps: Send POST /api/testcases with a body missing the required 'title' field.
expectedResult: Status 422. Response body identifies 'title' as the invalid/missing field.

title: Creating a test case with an invalid enum value for status returns 422
preconditions: Caller has a valid token with write permission.
expectedResult: Status 422. Response body lists the allowed values for 'status'.

title: Requesting a non-existent test case returns 404, not 200 with an empty body
preconditions: The given id does not exist.
expectedResult: Status 404. Response body contains a descriptive error message, not an empty object.

title: Sending a malformed JSON body returns 400, not a 500
preconditions: Caller has a valid token.
steps: Send POST /api/testcases with a syntactically invalid JSON body (e.g. a trailing comma or unclosed brace).
expectedResult: Status 400. The server does not crash or return a 500-series error; the response indicates the body could not be parsed.

title: An unauthenticated write attempt is rejected before any validation of the body
preconditions: None.
steps: Send POST /api/testcases with no Authorization header AND an invalid body (missing required fields).
expectedResult: Status 401 (authentication failure takes precedence over 422 body-validation failure). No resource is created.

title: An internal failure surfaces as a well-formed 5xx response, not a raw stack trace
preconditions: A dependency of the /api/testruns endpoint (e.g. its datastore) is simulated as unavailable, via test double or fault injection.
steps: With the dependency unavailable, send GET /api/testruns.
expectedResult: Status 5xx (e.g. 503). Response body is a well-formed JSON error object, not a raw stack trace or HTML error page.

title: Requests under the rate limit succeed normally
preconditions: Caller has a valid token. Assumed limit: 100 requests/minute per token.
steps: Send 50 GET /api/testcases requests within one minute using the same token.
expectedResult: All 50 requests return status 200.

title: Exceeding the rate limit returns 429 with a Retry-After header
preconditions: Caller has a valid token. Assumed limit: 100 requests/minute per token.
steps: Send 101 GET /api/testcases requests within one minute using the same token.
expectedResult: The 101st request returns status 429. The response includes a 'Retry-After' header with a positive integer value.

title: Rate limiting is scoped per token/user, not global
preconditions: Two valid tokens exist for two different users. Assumed limit: 100 requests/minute per token.
steps: Using token A, send 100 requests within one minute (reaching its limit).
Using token B, send 1 request within the same minute.
expectedResult: Token A's 101st request (if attempted) would return 429, but token B's request returns 200 — confirming the limit is per-caller, not shared across all users.

title: The rate limit window resets after the stated period
preconditions: Caller has already been rate-limited (received a 429).
steps: After receiving a 429, wait for the duration indicated in the 'Retry-After' header.
expectedResult: Status 200 — the request succeeds once the window has elapsed.

title: A created test case response includes all required fields with correct types
preconditions: Caller has a valid token with write permission.
steps: Send POST /api/testcases with a valid body.
Validate the response body against the expected schema.
expectedResult: Response includes: 'id' (string or integer), 'title' (string), 'status' (string, one of the documented enum values), 'priority' (string, one of the documented enum values), 'createdAt' (ISO 8601 date-time string). No required field is missing or null.

title: A listed test case in GET /api/testcases matches the single-resource schema
preconditions: At least one test case exists.
steps: Validate every item in the returned array against the same schema used for a single GET /api/testcases/{id}.
expectedResult: Every item in the list matches the single-resource schema — list and detail responses are not allowed to silently diverge (e.g. list omitting a field that detail includes).

title: An extra, unexpected field in the request body does not break the response schema
preconditions: Caller has a valid token with write permission.
steps: Send POST /api/testcases with a valid body plus one unexpected extra field, e.g. { title, priority, status, notARealField: 'x' }.
expectedResult: Status 201 (extra field is ignored) or 422 with a clear message about the unexpected field — either is acceptable as long as it's consistent and documented, but the response body still matches the required schema either way.

title: Numeric and date fields are not silently returned as strings
preconditions: At least one test run exists with a duration and a timestamp.
steps: Inspect the JSON types of the 'durationMs' and 'startedAt' fields.
expectedResult: 'durationMs' is a JSON number, not a numeric string. 'startedAt' is a valid ISO 8601 date-time string.
